data.title: MSG Sports to Explore Split of New York Knicks, Rangers in Bullish Move for Stock — Barrons.com
data.description:
by Andrew Bary

Investors have waited for Madison Square Garden Sports to make a shareholder-friendly move to help close the big gap between the company's market value and the value of the company's two sports teams — the New York Knicks and New York Rangers.

MSG Sports took a step in that direction Wednesday by saying it would explore a plan to separate the two teams through a tax-free spin-off.

That would create separate companies for the Knicks and Rangers and "enable shareholders to more clearly evaluate each company's assets and growth prospects, while providing both companies with enhanced strategic and financial flexibility," the company said in a statement early Wednesday.

MSG Sports stock jumped 13% in early trading Wednesday to $331 after gaining 0.8% Tuesday and hitting a new 52-week high. MSG Sports now is valued around $8 billion.

That's still below what some investors consider the combined value of the team, which could be close to $14 billion. But the gap is closing.

The Knicks could be worth around $10 billion, based on their status as one of the leading teams in the NBA. The Los Angeles Lakers were sold last year in a transaction that valued the team at $10 billion.

Forbes, which values professional sports teams, has put the Rangers' value at $4 billion. The team is one of the most valuable in the National Hockey League.

MSG Sports has long traded at a huge discount to the value of the two teams because of the perception that the controlling Dolan family would never sell the company, or either of the two teams. CEO James Dolan has reinforced that view with public statements. The company has talked about a potential partial sale of one of the two teams, but nothing has happened on that front.

MSG Sports also has modest earnings relative to its market value. It doesn't pay a regular dividend and hasn't bought back stock since its fiscal year 2023. The company has about $1 billion in annual revenue and is expected to have adjusted operating income of around $25 million, including non-cash lease expense, in the current fiscal year ending in June, according to Wolfe Research analyst Peter Supino.

Wednesday's move is the company's first major action to realize value in the teams.

There's a good chance that the split occurs, because the board of directors unanimously agreed to explore a spinoff. The board is controlled by the Dolan family, including CEO James Dolan. The Dolans control the company through super-voting shares.

Jon Boyar, president of the Boyar Value Group, has advocated for a spinoff. He wrote an open letter to James Dolan in June 2025 arguing that a split of the two teams into independent companies would "unlock billions in value for shareholders."

He argued that standalone entities would trade at a "material premium" to MSG Sports' then-current valuation and enable a "future monetization" in a "more tax-efficient manner."

"This is a great first step for the Dolan family toward ending the 'Dolan discount' at Madison Square Garden Sports," Boyar told Barron's on Wednesday.

"This proposed separation helps highlight that valuation discrepancy. It also makes it easier for an investor to buy a minority — or full — stake in either team," he added. He puts the combined value of the teams at around $14 billion.

It's possible that a spinoff could also be a prelude to a partial sale of one of the two teams, which could help narrow the gap between the MSG Sports' market value and the teams' combined value.

Write to Andrew Bary at andrew.bary@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: U.S. Housing Starts, Building Permits Exceed Estimates In December
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - A report released by the Commerce Department on Wednesday showed a sharp increase in new residential construction in the U.S. in the month of December.

The Commerce Department said housing starts spiked by 6.2 percent to an annual rate of 1.404 million in December after jumping by 3.9 percent to an annual rate of 1.322 million in November. Economists had expected housing starts to come in at an annual rate of 1.33 million.

The continued surge by housing starts partly reflected a significant increase in multi-family starts, which soared by 11.3 percent to an annual rate of 423,000 in December after rising by 0.5 percent to an annual rate of 380,000 in November.

Single-family starts also shot up by 4.1 percent to an annual rate of 981,000 in December after surging by 5.4 percent to an annual rate of 942,000 in November.

The Commerce Department also said building permits jumped by 4.3 percent to an annual rate of 1.448 million in December after slumping by 1.6 percent to an annual rate of 1.388 million in November.

Building permits, an indicator of future housing demand, were expected to come in at an annual rate of 1.400 million.

The report showed multi-family permits led the way higher, spiking by 15.2 percent to an annual rate of 567,000 in December after plunging by 7.7 percent to an annual rate of 492,000 in November.

Meanwhile, single-family permits slumped by 1.7 percent to an annual rate of 881,000 in December after jumping by 2.1 percent to an annual rate of 896,000 in November.

'We expect a gradual improvement in housing starts over the course of 2026 but that won't be apparent immediately,' said Nancy Vanden Houten, U.S. Lead Economist at Oxford Economics.

She added, 'January starts are likely to be weaker than the December reading due to the severe winter weather that affected much of the country.'

The National Association of Home Builders released a separate report on Tuesday showing homebuilder confidence has unexpectedly seen a modest deterioration in the month of February.

The report said the NAHB/Wells Fargo Housing Market Index edged down to 36 in February after slipping to 37 in January. Economists had expected the index to inch up to 38.

With the unexpected dip, the housing market index dropped to its lowest level since hitting 32 last September.

Copyright(c) 2026 RTTNews.com. All Rights Reserved

Copyright RTT News/dpa-AFX

data.title: Fiverr Shares Drop After Lower 4Q Earnings, Concern Over 2026 Revenue
data.description:
By Chris Wack

Fiverr International shares were 11% lower, at $11.72, after the company reported lower fourth-quarter earnings, and expressed uncertainty about its 2026 revenue.

The stock hit its 52-week low of $10.25 earlier in the session, and is down 64% in the past 12 months.

The company said it had quarterly earnings of $11.5 million, or 31 cents a share, compared with $12.8 million, or 33 cents a share, in the same quarter last year. Analysts polled by FactSet were looking for earnings of $13.7 million, or 35 cents a share.

Revenue for the quarter was $107.2 million, compared with last year's $103.7 million and the $109 million analysts were expecting.

The company said Marketplace revenue was $71.5 million, 2.7% lower than last year's $73.5 million. Annual active buyers as of Dec. 31 were 3.1 million, down 13.6% from last year's 3.6 million.

Adjusted earnings were 86 cents a share, compared with the 74 cents analysts were looking for.

Fiverr said it is expecting first-quarter revenue of $100 million to $108 million, and fiscal 2026 revenue of $380 million to $420 million. Analysts are looking for first-quarter revenue of $112.3 million and 2026 revenue of $456.9 million.

The company said the wider-than-normal revenue range reflects elevated uncertainty on its business, coupled with continued uncertainty around external market conditions.

Write to Chris Wack at chris.wack@wsj.com

data.title: Airbnb Rolls Out $750 Incentive for New Hosts in 2026 World Cup Cities
data.description:
By Connor Hart

Airbnb will offer new hosts up to $750 to list and rent out their homes in cities hosting matches during the 2026 FIFA World Cup, in what the company described as its largest new-host incentive program to date.

The short-term rental platform said Wednesday that searches for stays in the 16 host cities are up about 80% on average compared with the same period last year, citing rising demand ahead of the tournament.

Under the program, first-time hosts who list an entire home and complete their first stay by July 31 will be eligible for the $750 payment, in addition to rental income earned from guests. Deloitte estimates that residents in host cities could earn about $3,000 on average by renting their space during the event, according to Airbnb.

"There's truly never been a better time to become a host on Airbnb," Chief Business Officer Dave Stephenson said.

Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com

data.title: Travel & Leisure Co. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Travel & Leisure Co., a leading player in the leisure travel industry, has released its 2025 10-K report, providing a comprehensive overview of its financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces. The company, known for its Vacation Ownership and Travel and Membership segments, continues to navigate a competitive market while focusing on optimizing its resort portfolio and expanding its multi-brand strategy.

Financial Highlights

  • Net Revenues: $4,021 million, reflecting an increase of $157 million compared to 2024, primarily driven by increased revenues at the Vacation Ownership segment.
  • Operating Income: $553 million, a decrease of $180 million from 2024, impacted by increased expenses related to inventory write-downs and impairments.
  • Net Income Attributable to Travel & Leisure Co. Shareholders: $230 million, a decrease of $181 million from 2024, primarily due to higher expenses and lower operating income.
  • Basic Earnings Per Share: $3.51, down from $5.87 in 2024, reflecting the decrease in net income.
  • Diluted Earnings Per Share: $3.44, compared to $5.82 in 2024, indicating a decline in profitability per share.

Business Highlights

  • Vacation Ownership Segment: The segment, which includes brands like Club Wyndham and WorldMark, saw an increase in Gross VOI sales by 8.4% in 2025, driven by a 6.1% increase in volume per guest and a 2.5% increase in tours. This segment remains the largest in terms of revenue generation, with a focus on optimizing revenue potential through owner upgrades and leveraging relationships with brands like Wyndham Hotels.
  • Travel and Membership Segment: This segment experienced a decline in exchange transactions by 9.0% in 2025, attributed to a reduction in member counts and an increasing mix of exchange members with club affiliations. However, Travel Club transactions grew by 13.8%, indicating a shift in consumer preferences within this segment.
  • Geographical Performance: In 2025, 88% of the company's revenues were derived from the United States, with the remaining 12% coming from international operations, highlighting the company's strong domestic presence and reliance on the U.S. market.
  • Sales Units: The company reported 734,000 tours in the Vacation Ownership segment, reflecting a 2.5% increase from the previous year. This metric is crucial as it directly impacts the sales of vacation ownership interests.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to continue optimizing its resort portfolio through the Resort Optimization Initiative, which aims to align with evolving owner preferences and preserve the affordability of maintenance fees. This initiative is expected to positively impact Adjusted EBITDA starting in 2026.
  • Consumer Financing: The company offers financing to purchasers of VOIs, with 68% of VOI sales being financed in 2025. This financing strategy is supported by a strong loan portfolio, which was 94% current as of the end of 2025.
  • Operational Strategy: The company is focused on expanding its multi-brand strategy within the Vacation Ownership segment and enhancing its B2B travel solutions in the Travel and Membership segment to drive growth and capture a greater share of the leisure travel market.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Resort Optimization Initiative: In 2025, the company undertook a Resort Optimization Initiative to enhance the quality of its resort portfolio, aligning with owner preferences and reducing maintenance costs. This involved a strategic review that identified 17 resorts for significant reinvestment or removal from the portfolio. The initiative is expected to result in annual savings and a positive impact on Adjusted EBITDA starting in 2026.
  • Capital Management: The company focused on capital management by refinancing its revolving credit facility and Term Loan B facility, reducing interest rates and extending maturities. It issued $500 million in secured notes due 2033, using proceeds to redeem existing notes and reduce revolving credit borrowings. The company also repurchased 5.4 million shares for $300 million and paid $149 million in dividends, maintaining a consistent dividend growth strategy. The revolving credit facility was amended to reduce pricing spreads and extend the maturity date to 2030, with $893 million available as of year-end 2025.
  • Future Outlook: Looking ahead, the company plans to invest between $200 million and $230 million in vacation ownership development projects in 2026, ensuring adequate inventory for future sales. It anticipates spending $90 million to $100 million on capital expenditures for technology and facility enhancements. The company aims to continue its asset-light strategy by partnering with financial entities for strategic investments in development projects. Additionally, the Board increased the share repurchase program authorization by $750 million, reflecting a commitment to returning value to shareholders.

Challenges and Risks

  • Market Risks: The timeshare industry is highly competitive, with new developments and additional properties being added to rental and sharing platforms, potentially creating new competitors without corresponding increases in demand. This competition may reduce fee structures and impact profits.
  • Operational Risks: The company's travel club businesses have not grown as quickly as anticipated, and there is a reliance on marketing efforts to closed user groups. The success of these businesses depends on leveraging relationships with travel suppliers and adapting to changing customer requirements.
  • Regulatory Risks: The company is subject to numerous regulations, including those related to timeshare, consumer financing, data protection, and privacy. Changes in these regulations or increased enforcement could adversely affect operations.
  • Emerging Risks: The company is exposed to risks related to climate change and extreme weather events, which could impact the desirability of travel to certain locations and increase insurance costs. Additionally, geopolitical turmoil and increased cyber threats pose significant risks to operations and data security.
  • Financial Risks: The company is subject to risks related to its indebtedness, including increased interest rates and the availability of capital. Changes in tax laws, such as the introduction of a global minimum tax, could also impact financial results.

SEC Filing: Travel & Leisure Co. [ TNL ] - 10-K - Feb. 18, 2026

data.title: BAE sales hit record amid new global arms race
data.description:
The Typhoon fighter jet and warship maker said full-year underlying operating profit jumped 12% to £3.32bn, beating expectations of a 9% - 11% increase, with its order backlog hitting a record £83.6bn.

Sales surged by 10% to £30.66bn as growing threats from Russia and China, combined with a pivot away from Europe by US President Donald Trump forced governments to ramp up spending on military equipment. Orders rose 9% to £36.8bn boosted by orders from Turkey worth £4.6bn for 20 new Typhoon fighter jets, $3.3bn in electronic systems and $1.7bn for US combat vehicles.

BAE guided for a 9% - 10% increase in 2026 profits and sales growth of 7% - 9% from last year’s £30.6bn.

Free cash flow fell 14% to £2.16bn, but net debt was cut by 22% to £3.84bn.

Richard Hunter, Head of Markets at interactive investor said the results reflected "the unfortunate sign of the times that defence stocks are squarely back in fashion, as governments around the world look to protect their interests and lands from growing tensions. For shareholders, however, this has resulted in significant rewards".

"Headwinds may be few and far between but for investors, they need to be considered. The lack of a share buyback announcement could result in some minor disappointment even though the rationale is sound as the group diverts resources elsewhere for investment and debt reduction purposes," he added.

"The meteoric share price rise has left the valuation well above the recent multi-year averages, suggesting that the shares are not obviously cheap, while with punchier valuations come higher expectations and more pressure to keep growing earnings to stay in line. Supply chain issues and production delays can also be a cost of doing business, while some may be deterred from investing in the sector on ethical grounds."

"Even so, for the medium term at least the future looks bright for BAE. Equally, the geopolitical backdrop is a reminder that brittle relationships are seemingly never far away, ranging from potential and actual conflicts in the likes of Venezuela, between China and Japan and Russia and Ukraine. "

"BAE shares have risen by 52% over the last year, as compared to a gain of 20.4% for the wider FTSE100, and by 127% over the last three years. The resultant higher valuation has done little to deter investors, with the market consensus of the shares remaining at a buy and the price reaction at the open a further reflection of optimism for the foreseeable future.”

Reporting by Frank Prenesti for Sharecast.com

data.title: U.S. Durable Goods Orders Pull Back Much Less Than Expected In December
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - New orders for U.S. manufactured durable goods pulled back by much less than expected in the month of December, according to a report released by the Commerce Department on Wednesday.

The Commerce Department said durable goods orders slumped by 1.4 percent in December after spiking by an upwardly revised 5.4 percent in November.

Economists had expected durable goods orders to tumble by 2.3 percent compared to the 5.3 percent surge that had been reported for the previous month.

The pullback by durable goods orders largely reflected a steep drop in orders for transportation equipment, which plunged by 5.3 percent in December after soaring by 15.2 percent in November.

Orders for non-defense aircraft and parts led the way lower, plummeting by 24.9 percent in December after skyrocketing by 98.2 percent in November.

Excluding orders for transportation equipment, durable goods orders increased by 0.9 percent in December after climbing by 0.4 percent in November. Ex-transportation orders were expected to rise by 0.3 percent.

Orders for computer and electronic products shot up by 3.0 percent, while orders for primary metals jumped by 1.7 percent.

The Commerce Department also said orders for non-defense capital goods excluding aircraft, a key indicator of business spending, grew by 0.6 percent in December after climbing by 0.8 percent in November.

Shipments in the same category, which is the source data for equipment investment in GDP, increased by 0.9 percent in December after rising by 0.2 percent in November.

data.title: Snowstorm cuts power to 200,000 homes across Romania
data.description:
Heavy snow and rain across Romania left 200,000 homes without electricity on Wednesday, Energy Minister Bogdan Ivan said, with traffic blocked on motorways and national roads and dozens of trains delayed.

Public transport in the capital, Bucharest, was struggling under 40 cm (16 inches) of snow. Fallen trees halted road and rail traffic, schools closed in several towns and 10 ambulances in six counties were snowed in, the national emergency response agency said.

"Of the 200,000 homes affected so far, roughly 86,000 were reconnected to power, there are ... 266 towns affected," Ivan told reporters.

He added Romania's power consumption was 7.4GW on average, with surplus wind and hydro production being exported. Production and consumption were up 11% and 6% on the year, respectively, he said.

data.title: US manufacturing output posts biggest gain in 11 months in January
data.description:
U.S. factory production increased by the most in 11 months in January, offering hope for a manufacturing sector that has been squeezed by import tariffs and high interest rates.

Manufacturing output rose 0.6% last month, the largest gain since February 2025, after being unchanged in December, the Federal Reserve said on Wednesday.

Economists polled by Reuters had forecast production for the sector, which accounts for 10.1% of the economy, would rise 0.4%. Output in December was previously reported to have risen 0.2%.

Production at factories advanced 2.4% on a year-over-year basis in January. Manufacturing has been hobbled by President Donald Trump's sweeping tariffs, which business leaders say have raised costs for factories and consumers.

Trump has defended his punitive import duties as necessary to restore a long-declining domestic industrial base. The manufacturing sector lost more than 80,000 jobs in 2025. Some segments like technology have thrived amid an artificial spending boom.

Economists are optimistic the boost from AI will broaden to the rest of manufacturing, which they also expect to get a lift from tax cuts.

The increase in factory output last month occurred across the board. Durable goods manufacturing output rose 0.8%, with strong gains in nonmetallic mineral products, machinery, computer and electronic products, miscellaneous durable goods, as well as motor vehicles and parts, which rose for the first time since last August.

Nondurable goods manufacturing output rose 0.4%, lifted by gains in the production of paper, printing and support as well as chemicals, plastics and rubber products.

Mining output fell 0.2% after decreasing 0.9% in the prior month. Utilities production increased 2.1% as the tailwind from freezing weather persisted. That reading followed a 3.0% jump in December. Overall industrial production advanced 0.7% after gaining 0.2% in December. Industrial increased 2.3% on a year-over-year basis in January.

Capacity utilization for the industrial sector, a measure of how fully firms are using their resources, increased to 76.2% from 75.7% in December. It is 3.2 percentage points below its 1972–2025 average. The operating rate for the manufacturing sector rose four-tenths of a percentage point to 75.6%. It is 2.6 percentage points below its long-run average.

data.description:
